A **lieutenant**[[a]](#cite_note-2)[[b]](#cite_note-3) is a [junior commissioned officer](/wiki/Junior_officer "Junior officer") rank in the [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_forces "Armed forces") of many nations, as well as [fire services](/wiki/Fire_service "Fire service"), [emergency medical services](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services "Emergency medical services"), [security services](/wiki/Security_agency "Security agency") and [police](/wiki/Police "Police") forces.

The rank in armies and air forces is often subdivided into subcategories of seniority. In [English-speaking navies](/wiki/Comparative_navy_officer_ranks_of_Anglophone_countries "Comparative navy officer ranks of Anglophone countries"), lieutenants are often equivalent to the army rank of [captain](/wiki/Captain_(armed_forces) "Captain (armed forces)"); in other navies, the lieutenants are usually equal to their army counterparts.

*Lieutenant* may also appear as part of a title used in various other organisations with a codified command structure. It often designates someone who is "[second-in-command](/wiki/Second-in-command "Second-in-command")", and as such, may precede the name of the rank directly above it. For example, a "lieutenant master" is likely to be second-in-command to the "master" in an organisation using both ranks.

Political uses include [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor "Lieutenant governor") in various governments, such as the [viceregal representatives](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant Governor (Canada)") of the [Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") in [Canadian provinces](/wiki/Canadian_provinces "Canadian provinces"). In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), a [lord lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_lieutenant "Lord lieutenant") is the sovereign's representative in a county or [lieutenancy area](/wiki/Lieutenancy_area "Lieutenancy area"), while a [deputy lieutenant](/wiki/Deputy_lieutenant "Deputy lieutenant") is one of the lord lieutenant's deputies.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *lieutenant* derives from [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"); the *lieu* meaning "place" as in a position ([cf.](/wiki/Cf. "Cf.") [in lieu of](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/in_lieu_of "wikt:in lieu of")); and *tenant* meaning "holding" as in "holding a position"; thus a "lieutenant" is a placeholder for a superior, during their absence (compare the Latin *[locum tenens](/wiki/Locum_tenens "Locum tenens")*).

In the 19th century, British writers who considered this word either an imposition on the English language, or difficult for common soldiers and sailors, argued for it to be replaced by the [calque](/wiki/Calque "Calque") "steadholder". However, their efforts failed, and the French word is still used, along with its many variations (e.g. [lieutenant colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel "Lieutenant colonel"), [lieutenant general](/wiki/Lieutenant_general "Lieutenant general"), [lieutenant commander](/wiki/Lieutenant_commander "Lieutenant commander"), [flight lieutenant](/wiki/Flight_lieutenant "Flight lieutenant"), [second lieutenant](/wiki/Second_lieutenant "Second lieutenant") and many non-English language examples), in both the [Old](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") and the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

Pronunciation of *lieutenant* as [/lɛfˈtɛnənt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-lieutenant.ogg "File:En-uk-lieutenant.ogg") [*lef-TEN-ənt*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") is generally associated with the armies of [British Commonwealth](/wiki/British_Commonwealth "British Commonwealth") countries, while [/luːˈtɛnənt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-lieutenant.ogg "File:En-us-lieutenant.ogg") [*loo-TEN-ənt*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") is generally associated with the United States military.[[2]](#cite_note-ahd-4) The early history of the pronunciation is unclear; [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") spellings suggest that both pronunciations may have existed even then.[[3]](#cite_note-oed-5) The majority of sixteenth- and seventeenth-century sources show pronunciations with /[v](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_fricative "Voiced labiodental fricative")/ or /[f](/wiki/Voiceless_labiodental_fricative "Voiceless labiodental fricative")/, but [Bullokar](/wiki/William_Bullokar "William Bullokar") has /liu/.[[4]](#cite_note-6)

The rare [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") variant spelling *leuf* for [Modern French](/wiki/Modern_French "Modern French") *lieu* ('place') supports the suggestion that a final /[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")/ of the Old French word was in certain environments perceived as an [f].[[3]](#cite_note-oed-5) Furthermore, in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), the *lingua franca* of the era,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the letter ⟨v⟩ is used for both /u/ and /v/.
In [Royal Naval](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") (RN) tradition—and other English-speaking navies outside the United States—a reduced pronunciation [/ləˈtɛnənt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-lieutenant.oga "File:En-lieutenant.oga") [*lə-TEN-ənt*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") is used. This is not recognised as current by recent editions of the *[OED](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* (although the RN pronunciation was included in editions of the *OED* up until the 1970s).

## Military rank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Military rank")]

### Lieutenant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Lieutenant")]

The senior grade of lieutenant is known as first lieutenant in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and as lieutenant in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and the rest of the [English-speaking](/wiki/English_language "English language") world. In countries that do not speak English, the rank title usually translates as "lieutenant", but may also translate as "first lieutenant" or "senior lieutenant". The Israel Defense Forces rank *segen* (סגן) literally translates as "deputy", which is equivalent to a lieutenant. In the Finnish military there is a [senior lieutenant](/wiki/Senior_lieutenant "Senior lieutenant") grade that ranks above lieutenant and second lieutenant but below captain; it does not have an English equivalent. In Germany it is called *[Oberleutnant](/wiki/Oberleutnant "Oberleutnant")* (senior lieutenant).

#### Army rank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Army rank")]

Conventionally, armies and other services or branches that use army-style rank titles have two grades of lieutenant, but a few also use a third, more junior, rank. Historically, the "lieutenant" was the deputy to a "captain", and as the rank structure of armies began to formalise, this came to mean that a [captain](/wiki/Captain_(Land) "Captain (Land)") commanded a [company](/wiki/Company_(military_unit) "Company (military unit)") and had several lieutenants, each commanding a [platoon](/wiki/Platoon "Platoon"). Where more junior officers were employed as deputies to the lieutenant, they went by many names, including second lieutenant, sub-lieutenant, [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(rank) "Ensign (rank)") and [cornet](/wiki/Cornet_(military_rank) "Cornet (military rank)"). Some parts of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), including the [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery"), [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers") and [fusilier](/wiki/Fusilier "Fusilier") [regiments](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment"), used first lieutenant as well as second lieutenant until the end of the 19th century, and some British Army regiments still preserve cornet as an official alternative to second lieutenant.

There is great variation in the insignia used worldwide. In most English-speaking and Arabic-speaking countries, as well as a number of European and South American nations, full lieutenants (and equivalents) usually wear two stars (pips) and second lieutenants (and equivalents) one. An example of an exception is the United States, whose armed forces distinguish their lieutenant ranks with a silver bar for first lieutenant and a gold bar for second lieutenant.

#### Marine rank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Marine rank")]

The [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") and British [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines")[[87]](#cite_note-89) both use army ranks, while many former Eastern-Bloc marine forces retain the naval rank structure. Before 1999 the Royal Marines enjoyed the same rank structure as the army, but at a grade higher; thus a Royal Marine captain ranked with and was paid the same as a British Army major. This historical remnant caused increasing confusion in multi-national operations and was abolished.

#### Air force rank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Air force rank")]

See also: [Flight lieutenant](/wiki/Flight_lieutenant "Flight lieutenant")

While some air forces use the army rank system, the British [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") and many other [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") air forces use another rank system in which [flight lieutenant](/wiki/Flight_lieutenant "Flight lieutenant") ranks with an army captain and naval lieutenant, a [flying officer](/wiki/Flying_officer "Flying officer") ranks with an army lieutenant and a [pilot officer](/wiki/Pilot_officer "Pilot officer") with an army second lieutenant.

During the early days of the naval rank, a lieutenant might be very junior indeed, or might be on the cusp of promotion to captain; by modern standards, he might rank with any army rank between second lieutenant and lieutenant colonel. As the rank structure of navies stabilized, and the ranks of commander, lieutenant commander and sub-lieutenant were introduced, the naval lieutenant came to rank with an army captain (NATO OF-2 or US O-3).

The insignia of a lieutenant in many navies, including the Royal Navy,[[91]](#cite_note-93) consists of two medium [gold braid](/wiki/Gold_braid "Gold braid") stripes (top stripe with loop) on a [navy blue](/wiki/Navy_blue "Navy blue") or black background. Where in Myanmar Navy, they're Sub Lieutenant with the insignia of 2 gold stars. This pattern was copied by the United States Navy and various Air Forces for their equivalent ranks grades, except that the loop is removed (see [flight lieutenant](/wiki/Flight_lieutenant "Flight lieutenant")).

### Lieutenant commander

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Lieutenant commander")]

Main article: [Lieutenant commander](/wiki/Lieutenant_commander "Lieutenant commander")

Lieutenants were commonly put in command of smaller vessels not warranting a commander or captain: such a lieutenant was called a "lieutenant commanding" or "lieutenant commandant" in the United States Navy, and a "lieutenant in command" or "lieutenant and commander" in the Royal Navy. The USN settled on "lieutenant commander" in 1862, and made it a distinct rank; the Royal Navy followed suit in March 1914. The insignia of an additional half-thickness stripe between the two full stripes of a lieutenant was introduced in 1877 for a Royal Navy lieutenant of 8 years seniority, and used for lieutenant commanders upon introduction of their rank.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

### First lieutenant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: First lieutenant")]

Main article: [First lieutenant](/wiki/First_lieutenant "First lieutenant")

#### "First lieutenant" in naval use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: \"First lieutenant\" in naval use")]

The first lieutenant in the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") and other Commonwealth navies, is a post or appointment, rather than a rank. Historically the lieutenants in a ship were ranked in accordance with seniority, with the most senior being termed the "first lieutenant" and acting as the [second-in-command](/wiki/Second-in-command "Second-in-command"). Although lieutenants are no longer numbered by seniority, the post of "first lieutenant" remains. In minor war vessels, [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer") and [frigates](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") the first lieutenant (either a lieutenant or lieutenant-commander) is second in command, [executive officer](/wiki/Executive_officer "Executive officer") (XO) and head of the executive branch; in larger ships where a commander of the warfare specialization is appointed as the executive officer, a first lieutenant (normally a lieutenant-commander) is appointed as his deputy. The post of first lieutenant in a [shore establishment](/wiki/Shore_establishment "Shore establishment") carries a similar responsibility to the first lieutenant of a [capital ship](/wiki/Capital_ship "Capital ship").

In the U.S. Navy or U.S. Coast Guard the billet of first lieutenant describes the officer in charge of the [deck department](/wiki/Deck_department "Deck department") or division, depending upon the size of the ship. In smaller ships with only a single deck division, the billet is typically filled by an ensign while in larger ships with a deck department, consisting of multiple subordinate divisions, the billet may be filled by a lieutenant commander. On submarines and smaller Coast Guard cutters the billet of first lieutenant may be filled by a [petty officer](/wiki/Petty_officer "Petty officer").

### Second lieutenant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Second lieutenant")]

Main article: [Second lieutenant](/wiki/Second_lieutenant "Second lieutenant")

Second lieutenant is usually the most junior grade of commissioned officer. In most cases, newly commissioned officers do not remain at the rank for long before being promoted, and both university graduates and officers commissioned from the ranks may skip the rank altogether.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Third lieutenant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Third lieutenant")]

Main article: [Junior lieutenant](/wiki/Junior_lieutenant "Junior lieutenant")

#### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Philippines")]

The following military and paramilitary services had the grade of third lieutenant: In the American Period, the Philippine Constabulary (PC) and the Philippine Army (PA), for which there was no insignia; During the Japanese period, the Bureau of Constabulary (BOC), whose insignia was a white-metal half-diamond which resembled a "V"; and in the forties, by the Philippine Army and the Philippine Air Force (PAF), during the postcolonial republic

#### United States rank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: United States rank")]

In March 1813, the US Army created the rank of third lieutenant. The rank was used as the entry level officer rank for the Ordnance Department and the Corps of Artillery until March 1821.[[93]](#cite_note-95) Throughout the 19th century and until as late as World War II[[94]](#cite_note-96) the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") sometimes referred to [brevet](/wiki/Brevet_(military) "Brevet (military)") [second lieutenants](/wiki/Second_lieutenant "Second lieutenant") as "third lieutenants". These were typically newly commissioned officers for which no authorized second lieutenant position existed. Additionally, the [Confederate States Army](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army") also used "third lieutenant", typically as the lowest ranking commissioned officer in an infantry company.

Notably, the [United States Revenue Cutter Service](/wiki/United_States_Revenue_Cutter_Service "United States Revenue Cutter Service") used a simple officer rank structure with Captain, First, Second and Third Lieutenants, each of whom had distinct insignia. The title of Third Lieutenant, essentially equal to the rank of [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(rank) "Ensign (rank)"), existed until 1915 when the Service became the nucleus of the new [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard").[[95]](#cite_note-97) Because of the time required to fully establish this organization the rank continued for some time afterwards; the first Coast Guard aviator, [Elmer F. Stone](/wiki/Elmer_F._Stone "Elmer F. Stone"), was a third lieutenant until 1918.[[96]](#cite_note-98)

### Sub-lieutenant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sub-lieutenant")]

Main article: [Sub-lieutenant](/wiki/Sub-lieutenant "Sub-lieutenant")

In the Royal Navy, the commissioned rank of mate was created in 1840, and was renamed sub-lieutenant in 1860. In the US Navy, the rank was called [master](/wiki/Master_(naval) "Master (naval)") until 1883, when it was renamed [lieutenant, junior grade](/wiki/Lieutenant,_junior_grade "Lieutenant, junior grade"). In many navies, a sub-lieutenant is a naval [commissioned](/wiki/Commissioned_officer "Commissioned officer") or [subordinate officer](/wiki/Subordinate_officer "Subordinate officer"), ranking below a lieutenant, but in Brazil it is the highest non-commissioned rank, and in Spain it is the second highest non-commissioned rank. In Portugal, sub-lieutenant is the rank of a junior naval officer graduated from a civil university or promoted from a NCO rank, while the equivalent rank of an officer graduated in the naval academy is designated midshipman.

## Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other uses")]

### Police rank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Police rank")]

Main article: [Police rank](/wiki/Police_rank "Police rank")

#### France and the French Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: France and the French Union")]

The first French Lieutenant of Police, [Gabriel Nicolas de la Reynie](/wiki/Gabriel_Nicolas_de_la_Reynie "Gabriel Nicolas de la Reynie"), was appointed in Paris by Louis XIV on 15 March 1667 to command a reformed police force. He was later elevated to [Lieutenant-General of Police](/wiki/Prefecture_of_Police "Prefecture of Police"). In the 17th century, the term "lieutenant" corresponded to "deputy" (i.e. a person appointed to carry out a task). La Reynie was the deputy for policing duties of the Provost of Paris, the ceremonial representative of the King in Paris. In 1995, the rank of *lieutenant* was introduced in the [National Police](/wiki/National_Police_(France) "National Police (France)") as the first rank of the police officers scale.

#### United Kingdom and Commonwealth police forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: United Kingdom and Commonwealth police forces")]

The rank of Lieutenant was formerly used in areas outside of the Metropolitan Police. The adoption of standardized ranks across the United Kingdom has eliminated its use. A number of city and burgh police forces in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") used the rank of lieutenant (and detective lieutenant) between inspector and superintendent from 1812 to 1948. It was replaced by the rank of [chief inspector](/wiki/Chief_inspector "Chief inspector").[[97]](#cite_note-99) The Royal Newfoundland Constabulary (founded 1871) had the rank of lieutenant between staff sergeant and inspector until 1997. In Australia, Queensland's first police force (founded 1864) had second lieutenants and lieutenants between the ranks of sergeant and inspector-general.

#### United States police forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: United States police forces")]

The rank of police lieutenant is used in most medium or large police departments in the United States, where it is one rank above sergeant and two ranks above a regular police officer (three in departments with a corporal rank). It is roughly equivalent to an [inspector](/wiki/Inspector "Inspector") in the British and Canadian police forces. The usual role of a lieutenant is to carry out administrative duties and assist precinct commanders (normally a captain, or sometimes the local police chiefs). In smaller police departments, they may command a precinct itself. Lieutenants either command a watch (8-hour "shift") of regular officers or a special unit for operations or investigations (like a Robbery-Homicide squad). The typical rank insignia for a lieutenant is a single silver bar (like that of an Army or Marine Corps First Lieutenant) or a single gold bar (like that of an Army or Marine Corps Second Lieutenant). Some police departments split the rank of lieutenant into two separate grades.

### Political titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Political titles")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Canada")]

In Canada, the representative of the [Canadian monarch](/wiki/Canadian_monarch "Canadian monarch") in each province is called the [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant Governor (Canada)"). The Lieutenant Governor exercises all the royal prerogative powers that the monarch holds.

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: France")]

In [French history](/wiki/History_of_France "History of France"), "lieutenant du roi" was a title borne by the officer sent with military powers to represent the king in certain provinces. It is in the sense of a deputy that it has entered into the titles of more senior officers, [lieutenant general](/wiki/Lieutenant_general "Lieutenant general") and [lieutenant colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel "Lieutenant colonel").

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Spain")]

Similar to other monarchies, in Spain existed the office of "*lugarteniente*" ([Luogotenente](/wiki/Luogotenente "Luogotenente")), a King's representative with some royal powers such us government and justice. The last person to hold the office was Cardinal [Luis Manuel Fernández de Portocarrero](/wiki/Luis_Manuel_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_Portocarrero "Luis Manuel Fernández de Portocarrero"), who served as Lieutenant General of the Realm ([regent](/wiki/List_of_Spanish_regents "List of Spanish regents")) from 1 November 1700 to 18 February 1701.

In more recent times, the title of lieutenant has been used to refer to the second-in-command of an institution. For example, in the Spanish [Public Prosecutor's Office](/wiki/Prosecution_Ministry "Prosecution Ministry"), the second to the [Attorney General](/wiki/Spanish_Attorney_General "Spanish Attorney General") is the Lieutenant Attorney of the Supreme Court. Likewise, in many Spanish [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Spain "Municipalities of Spain"), there is the position of the Lieutenant Mayor (*teniente de alcalde*, commonly translated as [deputy mayor](/wiki/Deputy_mayor "Deputy mayor")).

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

The [British monarch](/wiki/British_monarch "British monarch")'s representatives in the counties of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") are called [Lords Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant "Lord Lieutenant"). The [Lord Lieutenant of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland") performed the function of [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland").

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: United States")]

The [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(United_States) "Lieutenant governor (United States)") is an official in [state governments](/wiki/State_governments_of_the_United_States "State governments of the United States") of 45 out of 50 [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). In most cases, the lieutenant governor is the highest officer of state after the [governor](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)"), standing in for the governor when they are absent from the state or temporarily incapacitated. In the event a governor dies, resigns, or is removed from office, the lieutenant governor typically becomes governor.

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), a [governor](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)") serves as the [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") and [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") in each of the fifty [states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") and in the five permanently inhabited [territories](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States"), functioning as both [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") therein.

### Other organisations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Other organisations")]

#### The Boys' Brigade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: The Boys' Brigade")]

Leaders, or officers of the Boys' Brigade, particularly in the United Kingdom, are ranked as lieutenants after having completed their formal training, before which they are ranked as warrant officers. Officers serving in staff or command posts are awarded the "brevet" rank of captain, these officers then revert to their lieutenancy after having completed their tour of duty.

#### National Civil Defence Cadet Corps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: National Civil Defence Cadet Corps")]

The rank of cadet lieutenant (CLT) is given to officer cadet trainees who have passed their officer's course. The rank insignia of CLT is a pip and a bar below it. CLTs may be promoted to the rank of senior cadet lieutenant (S/CLT), which has a rank insignia of a pip and two bars below it.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

#### The Salvation Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lieutenant&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: The Salvation Army")]

The [Salvation Army](/wiki/Salvation_Army "Salvation Army") also uses lieutenant to denote first time officers, or clergymen/women.
